source: CDC Learn the Signs. Act Early. — Milestone Moments booklet

milestones.text: Can briefly calm himself (may bring hands to mouth and suck on hand)
Begins to smile at people
Tries to look at parent
Coos, makes gurgling sounds
Turns head toward sounds
Pays attention to faces
Begins to follow things with eyes and recognize people at a distance
Begins to act bored (cries, fussy) if activity doesn’t change
Can hold head up and begins to push up when lying on tummy
Makes smoother movements with arms and legs

actEarly.text: Doesn’t watch things as they move
Can’t hold head up when pushing up when on tummy

milestones.text: Smiles spontaneously, especially at people
Likes to play with people and might cry when playing stops
Copies some movements and facial expressions, like smiling or frowning
Begins to babble
Babbles with expression and copies sounds he hears
Cries in different ways to show hunger, pain, or being tired
Lets you know if she is happy or sad
Follows moving things with eyes from side to side
Responds to affection
Watches faces closely
Reaches for toy with one hand
Recognizes familiar people and things at a distance
Uses hands and eyes together, such as seeing a toy and reaching for it
Holds head steady, unsupported
Pushes down on legs when feet are on a hard surface
May be able to roll over from tummy to back
Can hold a toy and shake it and swing at dangling toys
Brings hands to mouth
When lying on stomach, pushes up to elbows

actEarly.text: Doesn’t watch things as they move
Doesn’t push down with legs when feet are placed on a hard surface
Has trouble moving one or both eyes in all directions

milestones.text: Knows familiar faces and begins to know if someone is a stranger
Responds to other people’s emotions and often seems happy
Likes to play with others, especially parents
Likes to look at self in a mirror
Strings vowels together when babbling (“ah,” “eh,” “oh”) and likes taking turns with parent while making sounds
Responds to sounds by making sounds
Responds to own name
Makes sounds to show joy and displeasure
Begins to say consonant sounds (jabbering with “m,” “b”)
Looks around at things nearby
Brings things to mouth
Shows curiosity about things and tries to get things that are out of reach
Begins to pass things from one hand to the other
Rolls over in both directions (front to back, back to front)
When standing, supports weight on legs and might bounce
Begins to sit without support
Rocks back and forth, sometimes crawling backward before moving forward

actEarly.text: Doesn’t try to get things that are in reach
Doesn’t roll over in either direction
Doesn’t respond to sounds around him
Has difficulty getting things to mouth
Seems very floppy, like a rag doll
Doesn’t laugh or make squealing sounds
Seems very stiff, with tight muscles

milestones.text: May be afraid of strangers
Has favorite toys
May be clingy with familiar adults
Understands “no”
Makes a lot of different sounds like “mamamama” and “bababababa”
Copies sounds and gestures of others
Uses fingers to point at things
Watches the path of something as it falls
Moves things smoothly from one hand to the other
Looks for things he sees you hide
Picks up things like cereal o’s between thumb and index finger
Plays peek-a-boo
Puts things in her mouth
Stands, holding on
Can get into sitting position
Sits without support
Pulls to stand
Crawls

actEarly.text: Doesn’t bear weight on legs with support
Doesn’t play any games involving back-and-forth play
Doesn’t seem to recognize familiar people
Doesn’t transfer toys from one hand to the other

milestones.text: Is shy or nervous with strangers
Cries when mom or dad leaves
Has favorite things and people
Shows fear in some situations
Hands you a book when he wants to hear a story
Repeats sounds or actions to get attention
Puts out arm or leg to help with dressing
Plays games such as “peek-a-boo” and “pat-a-cake”
Responds to simple spoken requests
Says “mama” and “dada” and exclamations like “uh-oh!”
Uses simple gestures, like shaking head “no” or waving “bye-bye”
Tries to say words you say
Makes sounds with changes in tone (sounds more like speech)
Explores things in different ways, like shaking, banging, throwing
Finds hidden things easily
Bangs two things together
Starts to use things correctly; for example, drinks from a cup, brushes hair
Lets things go without help
Copies gestures
Pokes with index (pointer) finger
Puts things in a container, takes things out of a container
Follows simple directions like “pick up the toy”
Looks at the right picture or thing when it’s named
Gets to a sitting position without help
May take a few steps without holding on
Pulls up to stand, walks holding on to furniture (“cruising”)
May stand alone

actEarly.text: Doesn’t learn gestures like waving or shaking head
Doesn’t search for things that she sees you hide
Doesn’t say single words like “mama” or “dada”

milestones.text: Likes to hand things to others as play
May cling to caregivers in new situations
May have temper tantrums
Points to show others something interesting
Explores alone but with parent close by
May be afraid of strangers
Shows affection to familiar people
Plays simple pretend, such as feeding a doll
Says several single words
Says and shakes head “no”
Points to show someone what he wants
Knows what ordinary things are for; for example, telephone, brush, spoon
Points to get the attention of others
Scribbles on his own
Points to one body part
Shows interest in a doll or stuffed animal by pretending to feed
Can follow 1-step verbal commands without any gestures; for example, sits when you say “sit down”
Walks alone
Can help undress herself
May walk up steps and run
Drinks from a cup
Pulls toys while walking
Eats with a spoon

actEarly.text: Doesn’t point to show things to others
Doesn’t notice or mind when a caregiver leaves or returns
Doesn’t know what familiar things are for

milestones.text: Copies others, especially adults and older children
Shows defiant behavior (doing what he has been told not to)
Gets excited when with other children
Plays mainly beside other children, but is beginning to include other children, such as in chase games
Shows more and more independence
Points to things or pictures when they are named
Knows names of familiar people and body parts
Follows simple instructions
Repeats words overheard in conversation
Points to things in a book
Says sentences with 2 to 4 words
Finds things even when hidden under two or three covers
Might use one hand more than the other
Begins to sort shapes and colors
Follows two-step instructions such as “Pick up your shoes and put them in the closet.”
Completes sentences and rhymes in familiar books
Plays simple make-believe games
Builds towers of 4 or more blocks
Names items in a picture book such as a cat, bird, or dog
Stands on tiptoe
Kicks a ball
Climbs onto and down from furniture without help
Begins to run
Throws ball overhand
Walks up and down stairs holding on
Makes or copies straight lines and circles

actEarly.text: Doesn’t know what to do with common things, like a brush, phone, fork, spoon

milestones.text: Understands the idea of “mine” and “his” or “hers”
Shows a wide range of emotions
Takes turns in games
Separates easily from mom and dad
Shows concern for a crying friend
May get upset with major changes in routine
Copies adults and friends
Shows affection for friends without prompting
Dresses and undresses self
Follows instructions with 2 or 3 steps
Can name most familiar things
Understands words like “in,” “on,” and “under”
Says first name, age, and sex
Names a friend
Talks well enough for strangers to understand most of the time
Says words like “I,” “me,” “we,” and “you” and some plurals (cars, dogs, cats)
Carries on a conversation using
Can work toys with buttons, levers, and moving parts
Copies a circle with pencil or crayon
Turns book pages one at a time
Plays make-believe with dolls, animals, and people
Builds towers of more than
Does puzzles with 3 or 4 pieces
Screws and unscrews jar lids or turns door handle
Understands what “two” means
Climbs well
Runs easily
Walks up and down stairs, one foot on each step
Pedals a tricycle (3-wheel bike)

actEarly.text: Falls down a lot or has trouble with stairs
Drools or has very unclear speech
Can’t work simple toys (such as peg boards, simple puzzles, turning a handle)
Doesn’t want to play with other children or with toys

milestones.text: Enjoys doing new things
Plays “Mom” or “Dad”
Is more and more creative with make-believe play
Often can’t tell what’s real and what’s make-believe
Would rather play with other children than by himself
Talks about what she likes and what she is interested in
Cooperates with other children
Tells stories
Sings a song or says a poem from memory such as the “Itsy Bitsy Spider” or the “Wheels on the Bus”
Knows some basic rules of grammar, such as correctly using “he” and “she”
Can say first and last name
Names some colors and some numbers
Draws a person with 2 to 4 body parts
Understands the idea of counting
Uses scissors
Starts to understand time
Starts to copy some capital letters
Remembers parts of a story
Plays board or card games
Understands the idea of “same” and “different”
Tells you what he thinks is going to happen next in a book
Hops and stands on one foot up to 2 seconds
Catches a bounced ball most of the time
Pours, cuts with supervision, and mashes own food

actEarly.text: Resists dressing, sleeping, and using the toilet
Shows no interest in interactive games or make-believe
Ignores other children or doesn’t respond to people outside the family

milestones.text: Wants to please friends
Wants to be like friends
More likely to agree with rules
Likes to sing, dance, and act
Is aware of gender
Shows more independence (for example, may visit a next-door neighbor by himself [adult supervision is still needed])
Is sometimes demanding and sometimes very cooperative
Can tell what’s real and what’s make-believe
Tells a simple story using full sentences
Speaks very clearly
Uses future tense; for example, “Grandma will be here.”
Says name and address
Counts 10 or more things
Can print some letters or numbers
Can draw a person with at least
Knows about things used every day, like money and food
Copies a triangle and other shapes
Stands on one foot for 10 seconds or longer
Uses a fork and spoon and sometimes a table knife
Hops; may be able to skip
Can use the toilet on her own
Can do a somersault
Swings and climbs

actEarly.text: Doesn’t show a wide range of emotions
Doesn’t respond to people, or responds only superficially
Doesn’t talk about daily activities or experiences
Shows extreme behavior (unusually fearful, aggressive, shy, or sad)
Can’t tell what’s real and what’s make-believe
Doesn’t use plurals or past tense properly
Doesn’t play a variety of games and activities
Is easily distracted, has trouble focusing on one activity for more than 5 minutes
Can’t give first and last name
Can’t brush teeth, wash and dry hands, or get undressed without help
